records.name: UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 (privacy notice, transparency and marketing lawful basis)

citation.act: UK GDPR (assimilated Regulation (EU) 2016/679); Data Protection Act 2018
citation.section: UK GDPR Arts 5, 6, 13, 14, 21 (transparency, lawful basis, right to object to direct marketing); DPA 2018 Part 2

applies_when: processes personal data of UK residents (any business with a contact form, account area, analytics or a mailing list)
collects names, emails, IP addresses or payment data through the website
excluded_when: purely personal or household processing (not a business site)
genuinely no personal data collected at all (no forms, no accounts, no analytics, no cookies)

website_obligations.duty: Publish an accessible privacy notice carrying the Article 13/14 disclosures, reachable from every page
elements: identity and contact details of the controller
purposes of processing with the lawful basis stated per purpose (Art 6)
data subject rights route (access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection) with a working contact
retention periods or the criteria used to set them
international transfer mechanism named where personal data leaves the UK
right to complain to the ICO stated
for direct marketing, the right to object made explicit (Art 21)

website_obligations.duty: State a valid lawful basis for marketing (consent, or legitimate interests for existing-customer postal/limited channels) rather than relying on a bare privacy policy
elements: marketing lawful basis identified
no reliance on consent that is bundled, pre-ticked or non-granular

penalty.basis: Higher maximum of GBP 17.5m or 4% of total worldwide annual turnover (UK GDPR Art 83(5)); typical band from 2025 ICO monetary penalties

enforcement.summary: Fined GBP 14m (GBP 8m controller plus GBP 6m processor) after a March 2023 cyber attack exposed the personal data of around 6.6 million people; failure to implement appropriate security measures.

enforcement.case: ICO v Advanced Computer Software Group
enforcement.summary: First ICO fine of a data processor; ransomware attack exposed NHS patient data after the 2022 Adastra outage.
Fined GBP 2.31m for a 2023 credential-stuffing breach exposing genetic and other data; the ICO penalty notice (5 June 2025) concerned 155,592 UK residents, with 6.9m affected worldwide.

intel.why_matters: Almost every business website processes personal data, so the privacy notice is the single most universally binding obligation and the first thing the ICO and a data subject look for.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Show me your privacy notice and tell me your lawful basis for each processing purpose, especially marketing.
intel.relevance_hook: A missing, generic or copy-pasted privacy notice with no stated lawful basis is a visible, evidenced breach on the public page, before any breach even happens.

records.name: Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (cookie consent and electronic marketing)

citation.act: Privacy and Electronic Communications (EC Directive) Regulations 2003 (SI 2003/2426)
citation.section: reg 6 (storing/accessing information on terminal equipment - cookies); reg 22 (unsolicited electronic mail marketing)

applies_when: sets any non-essential cookie or similar technology (analytics, advertising, embedded media, tag managers)
sends marketing email or SMS, or runs a newsletter signup
excluded_when: only strictly necessary cookies are set (session, security, load balancing, cart)
marketing sent only to existing customers about similar products where the soft opt-in and an easy unsubscribe are both satisfied (reg 22(3))

(UK only) first-party statistical or analytics storage or access whose sole purpose is measuring how this site or service is used in order to improve it, where the collected information is not shared with any third party except a processor assisting those improvements, the technology does not rely on device fingerprinting (information automatically emitted by the device), and the site gives clear and comprehensive information and a simple means, free of charge, to object (PECR Schedule A1 para 5, inserted by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, in force 5 February 2026; no equivalent under EU or French PECR)

(UK only) storage or access whose sole purpose is adapting this site's appearance or functionality to the user's preferences, on the same clear-information and simple free-of-charge objection basis (PECR Schedule A1 para 6, inserted by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025)

website_obligations.duty: Obtain prior informed consent before setting non-essential cookies, with reject as easy as accept and no pre-consent firing
elements: consent banner blocks non-essential cookies until the user acts
a reject-all control as prominent and easy as accept-all
granular purpose controls, not a single accept button
no cookie wall that conditions access on acceptance
a cookie policy listing the cookies, purposes and durations

website_obligations.duty: Only send electronic marketing with consent or a valid soft opt-in, and give a working unsubscribe
elements: newsletter signup uses unbundled, non-pre-ticked consent
sender identifiable and an unsubscribe route present

penalty.basis: Historic PECR cap was GBP 500,000; the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 raises PECR maxima toward UK GDPR levels (up to GBP 17.5m or 4% turnover). Typical band from ICO PECR penalties.

enforcement.case: ICO v Grocery Delivery E-Services UK Ltd (HelloFresh)
enforcement.summary: Fined for 79m spam emails and 1m spam texts; consent was neither specific nor informed under PECR reg 22.

enforcement.case: ICO cookie compliance review of top 1,000 UK websites
enforcement.summary: ICO reviewing the top 1,000 UK sites for cookie compliance and issuing notices where reject-all is missing or cookies fire before consent.

intel.why_matters: Cookie banners and newsletter signups are on almost every commercial site, and the ICO is actively sweeping the top 1,000 sites, so this is high-visibility and easy to test from the page.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Do non-essential cookies fire before consent, and is reject-all as easy as accept-all?
intel.relevance_hook: A banner with only an accept button, or analytics that fire on load, is a breach anyone can see in the browser without asking the business a single question.

records.name: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (changes to UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR)

citation.section: Provisions amending UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR (including PECR penalty uplift and low-risk cookie exemptions); phased commencement by regulations

applies_when: sets cookies or does electronic marketing (PECR ceiling and cookie-exemption changes apply where in force)
processes UK personal data (recognised legitimate interests and other UK GDPR reforms apply where commenced)
excluded_when: provisions not yet commenced at the date of assessment (the Act commences in phases through regulations)

website_obligations.duty: Track DUAA commencement and reflect the raised PECR penalty ceiling and any narrowed cookie-consent exemptions for low-risk analytics as they come into force
elements: cookie banner still obtains consent for anything outside the newly exempt low-risk categories
privacy notice reflects any recognised legitimate interests relied on

penalty.basis: DUAA raises the PECR maximum toward UK GDPR levels (up to GBP 17.5m or 4% of worldwide turnover); figure applies once the relevant provision is commenced

enforcement.case: ICO PECR enforcement escalation following DUAA commencement
enforcement.summary: Following DUAA commencement the ICO can apply UK-GDPR-level maxima to PECR breaches and continues to review the top 1,000 UK sites for cookie compliance.

intel.why_matters: DUAA is the moving part of the UK data regime; the headline change owners feel is that a cookie or spam breach can now attract a UK-GDPR-scale fine rather than the old GBP 500k cap.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are you relying on a cookie exemption that DUAA actually grants, and does your banner still gate everything else?
intel.relevance_hook: The penalty for the same cookie-banner fault just went up by orders of magnitude, so a non-compliant banner is now a far bigger exposure than a year ago.

records.name: ICO data protection fee and register of fee payers (Data Protection (Charges and Information) Regulations 2018)

citation.act: Data Protection Act 2018 s.137; Data Protection (Charges and Information) Regulations 2018 (SI 2018/480)
citation.section: DPA 2018 s.137-138 (duty to pay charges); SI 2018/480 (fee tiers, register)

applies_when: is a controller processing personal data by automated means (nearly every UK business with staff, customers or a website)
excluded_when: processing falls entirely within an exemption (for example staff administration, advertising, accounts and records only, or a not-for-profit exemption) so no fee is due

website_obligations.duty: Pay the annual data protection fee and appear on the ICO public register of fee payers
elements: organisation is findable on the ICO register of fee payers under its legal name or number
correct tier paid (Tier 1 GBP 52, Tier 2 GBP 78, Tier 3 GBP 3,763 for 2024/25, subject to update)

penalty.basis: Non-payment attracts a penalty of up to 150% of the highest fee tier (up to about GBP 4,350) under the DPA 2018 fee regime

enforcement.summary: ICO routinely issues fixed monetary penalties to organisations that fail to pay the data protection fee, identified by cross-referencing Companies House against its register.

intel.why_matters: This is a binary, register-checkable duty: the business is either on the ICO register or it is not, and absence is an immediate hard finding needing no interpretation.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are you on our register of fee payers, and if not why do you say you are exempt?
intel.relevance_hook: A one-line register lookup proves the breach; it is the cleanest, least arguable compliance failure a website audit can surface.

records.name: Equality Act 2010 (accessible website as a reasonable adjustment; WCAG 2.2 AA evidence standard)

citation.section: s.20-21 (duty to make reasonable adjustments), s.29 (services and public functions: not to discriminate against a person requiring the service)

applies_when: provides goods, services or facilities to the public through the website (a service provider under s.29)
excluded_when: the site is not a service to the public (Public Sector Bodies Accessibility Regulations 2018 impose the stricter statutory WCAG duty on public bodies instead)

website_obligations.duty: Make reasonable adjustments so disabled users are not put at a substantial disadvantage; WCAG 2.2 AA is the recognised evidence benchmark
elements: text alternatives for non-text content (images have alt text)
sufficient colour contrast
full keyboard operability and visible focus
labels and instructions on forms
an accessibility statement is good practice and evidences intent
no reliance on an automated overlay as the sole measure

penalty.basis: No statutory cap; liability is uncapped compensation for injury to feelings and losses in a county court claim per claimant, plus EHRC action

intel.why_matters: Accessibility failures are testable from the page with automated and manual checks, and the duty is anticipatory: the business must have already made the site usable, not wait for a complaint.
intel.regulator_asks_first: What reasonable adjustments have you made so a disabled user can complete the same journey as everyone else?
intel.relevance_hook: Missing alt text, poor contrast and keyboard traps are measurable defects that map directly to a legal duty, and an overlay widget is not a defence.

sources: US accessiBe FTC action re overlay claims (seed, comparative)

records.name: Company and online trading disclosures (Companies Act 2006 s.82 and Electronic Commerce Regulations 2002 reg 6)

citation.act: Companies Act 2006 s.82; Company, Limited Liability Partnership and Business (Names and Trading Disclosures) Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/17); Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002 (SI 2002/2013)
citation.section: CA 2006 s.82-84 and SI 2015/17 regs 24-25 (trading disclosures); SI 2002/2013 reg 6 (information to be provided by an online service provider)

applies_when: is a UK-registered company or LLP, or any business providing an online service or selling online
excluded_when: a non-UK entity with no UK establishment and no company registration (the CA 2006 element does not apply, though reg 6 may still apply to a service provider established in the UK)

website_obligations.duty: Display the statutory company trading disclosures on the website
elements: full registered company name
company registration number
place of registration (for example Registered in England and Wales)
registered office address
LLP number where an LLP; sole traders show the proprietor name and a business address

website_obligations.duty: Provide the reg 6 online service-provider information in an easily, directly and permanently accessible form
elements: name of the service provider
geographic address where established
contact details including an email address
VAT number where the activity is subject to VAT
any trade register and registration number, and any professional body/authorisation details where regulated

penalty.basis: CA 2006 s.84: a fine on the company and every officer in default at level 3 on the standard scale (up to GBP 1,000); reg 6 breach is actionable and can support enforcement orders

regulator.name: Companies House / local authority Trading Standards

intel.why_matters: These are the simplest binary checks in any audit: the required identifiers are either printed in the footer or they are not, and their absence also undermines consumer trust and refund routes.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Where on your site do you show your registered company name, number and office address?
intel.relevance_hook: A footer missing the company number and registered office is a plain, non-arguable statutory breach that also signals a wider compliance gap.

records.name: Consumer Rights Act 2015 (fair and transparent terms; goods, services and digital content standards)

citation.section: Part 1 (goods, digital content, services: satisfactory quality, as described, reasonable care); Part 2 (unfair terms; transparency and the grey list at Sch 2)

applies_when: sells goods, services or digital content to consumers via the website
excluded_when: b2b only (business customers are outside the CRA consumer protections)

website_obligations.duty: Publish terms that are fair, transparent and legible, avoiding terms on the Schedule 2 grey list
elements: terms written in plain, intelligible language and available before purchase
no unfair terms that exclude statutory rights (satisfactory quality, fit for purpose, as described)
no term giving the trader an unfair unilateral right to vary price or content
digital content and services quality rights not excluded

penalty.basis: Unfair terms are unenforceable against the consumer; CMA/Trading Standards can seek undertakings or court orders and consumers can claim; no fixed statutory fine under Part 2

regulator.name: Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) / Trading Standards

intel.why_matters: Terms and conditions pages are read against the CRA grey list; an unfair or missing terms page both fails the law and exposes the trader to unenforceable contracts.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Are your consumer terms fair, transparent and free of terms that strip statutory rights?
intel.relevance_hook: Boilerplate terms lifted from a template often contain grey-list clauses that are automatically unenforceable, a defect visible on the terms page.

records.name: Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (B2C distance selling)

citation.act: Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (SI 2013/3134)
citation.section: Sch 2 (pre-contract information for distance contracts); reg 27-38 (14-day cancellation right and model cancellation form); reg 40 (additional payments require active consent)

applies_when: concludes distance contracts with consumers (online or off-premises sales of goods, services or digital content)
excluded_when: b2b only
specified exempt contracts (for example certain financial services, gambling, some passenger transport) which have their own regimes

website_obligations.duty: Give the prescribed pre-contract information before the consumer is bound, with the total price shown before payment
elements: main characteristics of the goods or services
total price including all taxes, or how it is calculated, plus delivery and any additional charges
trader identity, geographic address and contact details
the 14-day right to cancel and how to exercise it, with the model cancellation form
any exceptions to the cancellation right stated (for example bespoke goods or unsealed digital content)
the order button labelled to make clear payment is due (for example Order and pay)
no pre-ticked boxes for optional extra payments

penalty.basis: Breach can make the contract unenforceable, extend the cancellation period by up to 12 months, and support Trading Standards action; some breaches are criminal offences with a fine

regulator.name: Trading Standards / Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)

intel.why_matters: Every checkout for consumers is governed by these rules; a missing cancellation notice or a hidden total price is a direct, testable breach on the purchase flow.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Where in the buying journey do you state the 14-day cancellation right and the full inclusive price before payment?
intel.relevance_hook: An omitted cancellation notice silently extends the consumer's cancellation right to over a year, a costly, visible failure on the checkout page.

records.name: Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 (fake reviews, drip pricing and false urgency)

citation.act: Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024
citation.section: Part 4 Ch 1 (unfair commercial practices) and Sch 20 (banned practices, including fake/concealed-incentive reviews and hidden mandatory fees); commenced by SI 2025/272 on 6 April 2025

applies_when: markets or sells to consumers and shows prices, reviews, or urgency/scarcity messaging on the website
excluded_when: b2b only
subscription-contract transparency duties (Part 4 Ch 2) are not asserted until commenced (delayed beyond initial 2025 expectation)

website_obligations.duty: Do not publish, commission or fail to remove fake or incentivised consumer reviews without disclosure
elements: no fabricated or bought reviews
incentivised reviews clearly disclosed
reasonable steps taken to prevent and remove fake reviews on platforms

website_obligations.duty: Show the total price including any mandatory fees at the invitation to purchase (no drip pricing)
elements: unavoidable mandatory fees included in the headline price
no compulsory charges revealed only late in checkout

website_obligations.duty: Do not use false or misleading urgency or scarcity claims
elements: no fake countdown timers or false 'only X left' or 'sale ends' claims that are not true
no false claims of limited availability

penalty.basis: CMA can impose direct penalties up to 10% of global annual turnover for businesses (up to GBP 300,000 for individuals) without going to court

enforcement.case: CMA first DMCC direct consumer-enforcement investigations (online pricing)
enforcement.amount: Investigations opened (8 businesses; advisory letters to around 100 firms)
enforcement.summary: On 18 November 2025 the CMA opened its first direct consumer-protection enforcement investigations under the DMCC Act 2024 into eight businesses over a range of online pricing practices: mandatory/drip fees not included in the headline price (StubHub, viagogo, AA Driving School, BSM Driving School and Gold's Gym), misleading time-limited sales (Wayfair, and Appliances Direct) and automatic default opt-ins to optional charges (Marks Electrical, and Appliances Direct). It also issued advisory letters to around 100 further businesses across 14 sectors.

intel.why_matters: Since April 2025 the CMA can fine up to 10% of global turnover directly, and fake reviews, hidden fees and fake urgency are exactly the growth-hacking patterns many sites still use.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is every mandatory fee in your headline price, and is every countdown timer and 'X left' claim actually true?
intel.relevance_hook: A fake countdown timer, an undisclosed incentivised review, or a booking fee that only appears at step 3 are all now directly finable behaviours visible on the page.

records.name: Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading (misleading actions and omissions baseline)

citation.act: Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1277), now read with the DMCC Act 2024 Part 4
citation.section: regs 5-6 (misleading actions and omissions), reg 7 (aggressive practices), Sch 1 (banned practices); superseded for practices after 6 April 2025 by DMCC Part 4 Ch 1

applies_when: makes any commercial claim to consumers on the website (product claims, testimonials, endorsements, comparisons)
excluded_when: b2b only (governed instead by the Business Protection from Misleading Marketing Regulations 2008)

website_obligations.duty: Do not make misleading claims or omit material information that the consumer needs to make an informed decision
elements: no false or deceptive statements about the product, price or the business
no undisclosed paid endorsements or hidden commercial intent
material information (for example who you are, total price, key restrictions) not omitted or hidden

penalty.basis: Unlimited fine on conviction and up to 2 years imprisonment for the most serious offences; post-April-2025 conduct enforced under DMCC with direct CMA penalties

regulator.name: Trading Standards / Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)

enforcement.summary: CMA action against fake and misleading reviews on online platforms under the CPRs, with powers extended by the DMCC Act.

intel.why_matters: This is the general anti-deception backstop behind more specific rules; any exaggerated claim, hidden ad, or omitted material fact on the site can be a breach.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Can you substantiate every claim, and is every paid endorsement disclosed?
intel.relevance_hook: Unsubstantiated superlatives and undisclosed sponsored content are common and directly actionable, and now sit under the CMA's stronger DMCC powers.

records.name: UK Code of Non-broadcast Advertising and Direct & Promotional Marketing (CAP Code)

citation.act: UK Code of Non-broadcast Advertising and Direct & Promotional Marketing (CAP Code, 12th edition) - self-regulatory, backed by the CPRs/DMCC via CMA/Trading Standards referral
citation.section: CAP Code rules 1-3 (misleadingness, substantiation), rule 2 (recognition of marketing communications)

applies_when: publishes marketing communications on its own website and in paid or owned digital channels
excluded_when: purely editorial content with no marketing purpose

website_obligations.duty: Ensure marketing is legal, decent, honest and truthful, with claims capable of substantiation and ads clearly identifiable
elements: objective claims held with documentary evidence before publication
advertising and sponsored content clearly identifiable as marketing (for example ad or paid labels)
no misleading price, comparison or environmental claims

penalty.basis: ASA sanctions are non-monetary (ruling published, ad removal, ad alerts, paid-search removal, referral to Trading Standards/CMA where the CPRs/DMCC then apply monetary penalties)

regulator.name: Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) / Committee of Advertising Practice (CAP)

intel.why_matters: The CAP Code governs the claims on a business's own website; while ASA sanctions are reputational, unsubstantiated claims also breach the statutory CPRs/DMCC that sit behind it.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Where is the evidence for this claim, and is this content clearly marked as advertising?
intel.relevance_hook: An upheld ASA ruling is public and searchable, and the same claim can then be escalated to the CMA where real fines apply.

records.name: Online Safety Act 2023 (user-to-user and search services duties)

citation.section: Part 3 (duties of care for user-to-user and search services); Part 7 (Ofcom enforcement)

applies_when: operates a user-to-user service (users can post or share content others see: forums, comments, reviews, chat, user uploads) or a search service with UK users
excluded_when: the site hosts no user-generated content that other users can encounter (a brochure site with no forums, comments, reviews or uploads is out of scope)
limited-functionality services and certain internal/enterprise tools within the Act's exemptions

website_obligations.duty: Where in scope, complete an illegal-content risk assessment and, if children can access, a children's risk assessment, and put proportionate safety measures and reporting in place
elements: illegal content risk assessment completed and safety measures implemented
easy-to-find and use content reporting and complaints mechanism
clear terms of service on prohibited content and moderation
age assurance where the service is likely to be accessed by children and carries harmful content

penalty.basis: Ofcom can fine the greater of GBP 18m or 10% of qualifying worldwide revenue, and seek business disruption measures

intel.why_matters: Many ordinary business sites unknowingly fall in scope the moment they add a comments section, review widget or forum, which triggers real Ofcom duties.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Can users post content others see, and if so where is your risk assessment and reporting mechanism?
intel.relevance_hook: A single unmoderated comments or reviews feature can pull a small business into the Online Safety Act's duty-of-care regime, which most owners do not realise.

sources: NEON-UK-OSA-2023 (seed, corrected: activity-gated, penalty = greater of GBP 18m or 10% qualifying worldwide revenue)

records.name: Modern Slavery Act 2015 s.54 (transparency in supply chains statement)

citation.section: s.54 (transparency in supply chains); threshold set by the Modern Slavery Act 2015 (Transparency in Supply Chains) Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/1833)

applies_when: is a commercial organisation supplying goods or services, carrying on business in the UK, with a total annual turnover of GBP 36m or more
excluded_when: total annual turnover below GBP 36m (the vast majority of SMEs are out of scope and must not be flagged)

website_obligations.duty: Publish an annual slavery and human trafficking statement, approved by the board and signed by a director, with a prominent link on the homepage
elements: statement for the latest financial year published on the UK website
a link to the statement in a prominent place on the homepage
board approval and director signature
statement also submitted to the government modern slavery statement registry

penalty.basis: No financial penalty; the Secretary of State may seek an injunction to compel publication, and non-publication is a reputational and procurement risk

intel.why_matters: For large businesses the homepage link to a current statement is a hard, binary check; for SMEs the value is knowing the threshold so the audit does not raise a false flag.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is your turnover GBP 36m or more, and if so where is the prominent homepage link to this year's statement?
intel.relevance_hook: A qualifying business with no homepage link, or a stale prior-year statement, is a clean finding; a sub-threshold SME must be correctly excluded.

records.name: Price and VAT display to consumers (Price Marking Order 2004 and VAT-inclusive pricing)

citation.act: Price Marking Order 2004 (SI 2004/102); Value Added Tax Act 1994; read with CCRs 2013 and DMCC Act 2024 on total price
citation.section: Price Marking Order 2004 arts 4-7 (selling price and unit price, inclusive of VAT, to consumers)

applies_when: indicates prices of products to consumers on the website
excluded_when: b2b only, where VAT-exclusive prices may be shown to business customers
services rather than products may fall outside the unit-price element of the Order

website_obligations.duty: Show a clear selling price inclusive of VAT to consumers, with a unit price where required
elements: prices to consumers shown inclusive of VAT
unit price displayed for products sold by quantity where the Order requires it
prices unambiguous, legible and clearly associated with the product
any mandatory delivery or service charges made clear (reinforced by the DMCC total-price rule)

penalty.basis: Trading Standards can prosecute breaches of the Price Marking Order, typically a fine; DMCC drip-pricing penalties (up to 10% turnover) apply where mandatory fees are hidden

regulator.name: Trading Standards / Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)

intel.why_matters: Consumers must see the VAT-inclusive price up front; showing ex-VAT prices to consumers or hiding VAT until checkout is both a Price Marking Order and a total-price failure.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is the price a consumer sees inclusive of VAT and all unavoidable charges?
intel.relevance_hook: An ex-VAT headline price on a consumer product, or VAT added only at checkout, is a visible pricing breach that also overlaps the DMCC drip-pricing ban.

records.name: UK GDPR international data transfers (restricted transfers of personal data to a third country outside the UK)

citation.act: UK GDPR (assimilated Regulation (EU) 2016/679), Chapter V, as amended by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025; Data Protection Act 2018 Part 2
citation.section: Arts 44A (general principles for transfers), 45A (transfers approved by adequacy regulations), 46 (appropriate safeguards, including the ICO International Data Transfer Agreement and the UK Addendum), 47A (standard data protection clauses) and 49 (derogations); higher-tier penalty under Art 83(5)(c). Chapter V substituted and amended by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 s.142 and Sch 7, in force 5 February 2026 (SI 2026/82)

applies_when: personal data of UK residents reaches a receiver outside the UK (a third country or international organisation), including through overseas hosting, content-delivery, advertising, audience-measurement, tag-management or embedded-media services that receive IP addresses or other online identifiers
a processor, parent, affiliate or supplier located outside the UK stores or accesses UK personal data collected through the website
excluded_when: the receiver is covered by UK adequacy regulations under Art 45A (for example the EEA, or a US organisation self-certified to the UK Extension to the EU-US Data Privacy Framework under the Data Protection (Adequacy) (United States of America) Regulations 2023, in force 12 October 2023), so no additional safeguard is required and a third-country receiver alone does not prove an unlawful transfer

an appropriate safeguard under Art 46 is already in place (the ICO International Data Transfer Agreement, the UK Addendum to the EU standard contractual clauses, or standard data protection clauses under Art 47A) supported by a transfer risk assessment
an Art 49 derogation genuinely applies (for example the individual's explicit consent, or a transfer necessary for a contract with the individual)
processing and hosting are intra-UK only, with no personal data reaching any receiver outside the UK

website_obligations.duty: Where website personal data reaches a receiver outside the UK, rely on a valid Chapter V transfer route (adequacy regulations, an Art 46 appropriate safeguard such as the ICO International Data Transfer Agreement or Addendum, or an Art 49 derogation); an observed third-country receiver of personal data is a signal to review the transfer basis, not by itself proof of an unlawful transfer
elements: each third-country receiver of personal data (overseas hosting, advertising, tag-management, content-delivery or embedded-media provider) mapped to a lawful transfer route
adequacy relied on only where Art 45A adequacy regulations actually cover the receiver, for example a US recipient self-certified to the UK Extension to the EU-US Data Privacy Framework
where no adequacy applies, an Art 46 safeguard (the ICO International Data Transfer Agreement or Addendum) in place with a transfer risk assessment recorded
the privacy notice names the international transfer and the specific mechanism relied on

penalty.basis: Higher maximum of GBP 17,500,000 or 4% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher (UK GDPR Art 83(5)(c), covering transfers under Arts 44A to 49). The ICO has not issued a monetary penalty for a pure Chapter V transfer breach, so the modelled band reflects the ICO's cross-border and higher-tier practice (the GBP 7,552,800 Clearview AI fine, 2022) and the reprimand-first reality for transfer issues; the landmark restricted-transfer penalty is the EU/EDPB Meta decision (EUR 1.2bn, May 2023).

enforcement.case: ICO v Clearview AI Inc (Upper Tribunal judgment)
enforcement.summary: Illustrates ICO enforcement over the cross-border processing of UK residents' personal data by a US company: the ICO fined Clearview AI Inc GBP 7,552,800 in May 2022 for scraping UK residents' images into a global facial-recognition database. The fine rested on lawful basis and transparency rather than the Chapter V export-mechanism rules; after appeals, the Upper Tribunal (October 2025) upheld three of the ICO's four grounds. The archetypal restricted-transfer penalty remains the EU/EDPB Meta decision (EUR 1.2bn, 22 May 2023), an EU action noted for context, not UK enforcement.

intel.why_matters: Almost every website sends personal data abroad the moment it loads a US-hosted advertising, hosting or embedded-media tool, and since the Data (Use and Access) Act reforms (in force 5 February 2026) that transfer needs a lawful Chapter V route unless the receiver is covered by UK adequacy such as the UK Extension to the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Which of your website tools send personal data outside the UK, and for each one, is it adequacy-covered, on an Art 46 safeguard with a transfer risk assessment, or relying on an Art 49 derogation?
intel.relevance_hook: A US-hosted tracker or pixel receiving a visitor's IP address is a visible international-transfer signal to review; it is only lawful if the receiver is adequacy-certified (for example under the UK Extension to the EU-US Data Privacy Framework) or covered by an appropriate safeguard, which is why this needs review rather than an automatic breach.

sources: legislation.gov.uk UK GDPR Art 44A (general principles for transfers), verified 2026-07-19 (Chapter V substituted by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 s.142 and Sch 7, in force 5 February 2026)
legislation.gov.uk UK GDPR Art 46 and Art 47A (appropriate safeguards; standard data protection clauses) and Art 49 (derogations), verified 2026-07-19

Comparative context (not UK enforcement): EU/EDPB Meta Platforms Ireland Chapter V transfer decision, EUR 1.2bn, 22 May 2023

records.name: UK GDPR security of processing (encryption in transit and secure transport of personal data submitted through the website)

citation.act: UK GDPR (assimilated Regulation (EU) 2016/679); Data Protection Act 2018 Part 2
citation.section: Art 32 (security of processing, including the encryption of personal data) read with Art 5(1)(f) (integrity and confidentiality); standard-tier penalty under Art 83(4)

applies_when: collects personal data from visitors through the website (contact, account, enquiry, checkout or login forms)
transmits personal data between the browser and the server, or on to a payment or back-end service
excluded_when: the website collects no personal data at all (no forms, accounts, logins or payment fields)

personal data is transmitted only over an encrypted connection with no insecure or mixed-content transport path, and the controller's own risk assessment shows the transport security is appropriate to the risk

website_obligations.duty: Transmit personal data over the website using appropriate transport security (an encrypted HTTPS/TLS connection) so it is not sent in plaintext; a form on a secure page that submits to an insecure http endpoint, or mixed insecure content on a data-collecting page, is a transport-security risk indicator to review against the controller's own Art 32 risk assessment, not an automatic breach
elements: personal-data forms submit over an encrypted https connection, not to a plaintext http action
no insecure or mixed-content transport path carries personal data on a page that collects it
transport uses current secure protocols (TLS, not deprecated SSL) consistent with the ICO encryption-in-transit guidance
the level of transport security is matched to the risk of the personal data collected, as the controller's Art 32 assessment records

penalty.basis: Standard maximum of GBP 8,700,000 or 2% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher (UK GDPR Art 83(4), covering the Art 32 security obligations). Typical band from ICO Art 32 and Art 5(1)(f) security penalties: GBP 60,000 (DPP Law, 2025), GBP 98,000 (Tuckers Solicitors, 2022), GBP 1,228,283 (LastPass UK, 2025), GBP 3.07m (Advanced, 2025) and GBP 4.4m (Interserve, 2022); large-scale breaches reach higher through the 2% turnover route (British Airways GBP 20m and Marriott GBP 18.4m, 2020).

enforcement.summary: Penalty of GBP 1,228,283 issued to password-management provider LastPass UK Ltd on 20 November 2025 for infringements of Art 5(1)(f) and Art 32(1)(f) UK GDPR: a failure to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to keep personal data secure.

enforcement.case: ICO v Advanced Computer Software Group Ltd
enforcement.summary: Fined GBP 3.07m on 27 March 2025 for security failings that put the personal data of 79,404 people at risk; a 2022 ransomware attack reached a subsidiary's systems through a customer account with no multi-factor authentication (inadequate Art 32 technical measures).

Merseyside law firm DPP Law Ltd fined GBP 60,000 (16 April 2025) after a cyber attack led to a breach of highly sensitive personal data, following a failure to keep personal data secure under the UK GDPR security duty.

intel.why_matters: Every form that collects a name, email or payment detail must be transmitted securely; a page that submits personal data over plaintext http, or mixes insecure content, exposes that data in transit and is one of the clearest technical-security failings the ICO can point to.
intel.regulator_asks_first: Is every form that collects personal data submitted over an encrypted connection, and where is your assessment that the transport security is appropriate to the risk?
intel.relevance_hook: A login or checkout form that posts to an insecure http endpoint, or mixed insecure content on a data page, is a visible transport-security risk anyone can observe in the browser; it needs review against the site's own security assessment rather than an automatic verdict.

sources: legislation.gov.uk UK GDPR Art 32 (security of processing; the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data), verified 2026-07-19
legislation.gov.uk UK GDPR Art 83(4) standard-tier penalty (Arts 25 to 39 including Art 32; GBP 8,700,000 or 2%), verified 2026-07-19
ICO guidance 'Encryption and data transfer' (encryption in transit; HTTPS/TLS, not SSL), verified 2026-07-19
ICO LastPass UK Ltd penalty (Art 5(1)(f) and Art 32(1)(f), 20 November 2025), Advanced (27 March 2025) and DPP Law (16 April 2025) enforcement pages, verified 2026-07-19

DPA 2018 s.57 checked and NOT cited: it is 'data protection by design and default' in Part 3 (law-enforcement processing), not the general-processing security provision; general-processing security is UK GDPR Art 32 given domestic effect by DPA 2018 Part 2
